question:
Let  $f(x)$  be  a continuous real-valued function defined on the interval
$[0,1]$. Show that
\[
   \int_0^1 \int_0^1 | f(x) + f(y) |\,dx\,dy \geq \int_0^1 |f(x)|\,dx.
\]

\end{itemize}
\end{document}
solution:
\textbf{First solution:} (composite of solutions by Feng Xie and David
Pritchard)
Let $\mu$ denote Lebesgue measure on $[0,1]$. Define
\begin{align*}
E_+ &= \{x \in [0,1]: f(x) \geq 0\} \\
E_- &= \{x \in [0,1]: f(x) < 0\};
\end{align*}
then $E_+$, $E_-$ are measurable and $\mu(E_+) + \mu(E_-) = 1$.
Write $\mu_+$ and $\mu_-$ for $\mu(E_+)$ and $\mu(E_-)$.
Also define
\begin{align*}
I_+ &= \int_{E_+} |f(x)|\,dx \\
I_- &= \int_{E_-} |f(x)|\,dx,
\end{align*}
so that $\int_0^1 |f(x)|\,dx = I_+ + I_-$.

From the triangle inequality $|a+b| \geq \pm(|a| - |b|)$,
we have the inequality
\begin{align*}
&\iint_{E_+ \times E_-} |f(x) + f(y)|\,dx\,dy \\
&\geq
\pm \iint_{E_+ \times E_-} (|f(x)| - |f(y)|)\,dx\,dy \\
&= \pm ( \mu_- I_+ - \mu_+ I_-),
\end{align*}
and likewise with $+$ and $-$ switched. Adding these inequalities together
and allowing all possible choices of the signs, we get
\begin{align*}
&\iint_{(E_+ \times E_-) \cup (E_- \times E_+)} |f(x) + f(y)|\,dx\,dy \\
&\geq
\max\left\{ 0, 2 (\mu_- I_+ - \mu_+ I_-), 2 (\mu_+ I_- - \mu_- I_+) \right\}.
\end{align*}
To this inequality, we add the equalities
\begin{align*}
\iint_{E_+ \times E_+} |f(x) + f(y)|\,dx\,dy &= 2 \mu_+ I_+ \\
\iint_{E_- \times E_-} |f(x) + f(y)|\,dx\,dy &= 2 \mu_- I_- \\
-\int_0^1 |f(x)|\,dx &= -(\mu_+ + \mu_-)(I_+ + I_-)
\end{align*}
to obtain
\begin{multline*}
\int_0^1 \int_0^1 |f(x)+f(y)|\,dx\,dy - \int_0^1 |f(x)|\,dx \\
\geq \max\{ (\mu_+ - \mu_-)(I_+ + I_-)+ 2\mu_-(I_- - I_+), \\
(\mu_+ - \mu_-)(I_+ - I_-), \\
(\mu_- - \mu_+)(I_+ + I_-)+ 2\mu_+(I_+ - I_-) \}.
\end{multline*}
Now simply note that for each of the possible comparisons between
$\mu_+$ and $\mu_-$, and between $I_+$ and $I_-$, one of the three
terms above is manifestly nonnegative. This yields the desired result.

\textbf{Second solution:}
We will show at the end that it
is enough to prove a discrete analogue: if $x_1, \dots, x_n$
are real numbers, then
\[
\frac{1}{n^2} \sum_{i,j=1}^n |x_i + x_j| \geq \frac{1}{n}
\sum_{i=1}^n |x_i|.
\]
In the meantime, we concentrate on this assertion.

Let $f(x_1, \dots, x_n)$ denote the difference between the two sides.
We induct
on the number of nonzero values of $|x_i|$. We leave for later the base case,
where there is at most one such value. Suppose instead for now that there
are two or more. Let $s$ be the smallest, and suppose without loss of
generality that $x_1 = \cdots = x_a = s$, $x_{a+1} = \cdots = x_{a+b} = -s$,
and for $i > a+b$, either $x_i = 0$ or $|x_i| > s$. (One of $a,b$ might be
zero.)

Now consider
\[
f(\overbrace{t, \cdots,t}^{\mbox{$a$ terms}},\overbrace{-t,\cdots,-t}^{\mbox{$b$ terms}},x_{a+b+1},\cdots, x_n)
\]
as a function of $t$.
It is piecewise linear near $s$; in fact, it is
linear between 0 and
the smallest nonzero value among $|x_{a+b+1}|, \dots, |x_n|$
(which exists by hypothesis). Thus its minimum is achieved by one (or both)
of those two endpoints. In other words, we can reduce the
number of distinct nonzero absolute values among the $x_i$ without
increasing $f$. This
yields the induction, pending verification of the base case.

As for the base case, suppose that $x_1 = \cdots = x_a = s > 0$,
$x_{a+1} = \cdots = x_{a+b} = -s$, and $x_{a+b+1} = \cdots = x_n = 0$.
(Here one or even both of $a,b$ could be zero, though the latter case
is trivial.)
Then
\begin{multline*}
f(x_1, \dots, x_n) = \frac{s}{n^2} (2a^2 + 2b^2 + (a+b)(n-a-b)) \\
- \frac{s}{n} (a+b) = \frac{s}{n^2} (a^2 -2ab + b^2) \geq 0.
\end{multline*}
This proves the base case of the induction, completing the solution
of the discrete analogue.

To deduce the original statement from the discrete analogue,
approximate both integrals
by equally-spaced Riemann sums and take limits. This works because
given a continuous function
on a product of closed intervals,
any sequence of Riemann sums with mesh size tending to zero
converges to the integral. (The domain is compact, so
the function is uniformly continuous. Hence for any $\epsilon > 0$
there is a cutoff below
which any mesh size forces the
discrepancy between the Riemann sum and the integral to be less than
$\epsilon$.)

Alternate derivation (based on a solution by Dan Bernstein):
from the discrete analogue, we have
\[
\sum_{1 \leq i<j\leq n} |f(x_i) + f(x_j)| \geq \frac{n-2}{2}
\sum_{i=1}^n |f(x_i)|,
\]
for all $x_1, \dots, x_n \in [0,1]$. Integrating both sides as
$(x_1, \dots, x_n)$ runs over
$[0,1]^n$ yields
\begin{align*}
&\frac{n(n-1)}{2} \int_0^1 \int_0^1 |f(x)+f(y)|\,dy\,dx \\
&\geq
\frac{n(n-2)}{2} \int_0^1 |f(x)|\,dx,
\end{align*}
or
\[
\int_0^1 \int_0^1 |f(x)+f(y)|\,dy\,dx
\geq
\frac{n-2}{n-1} \int_0^1 |f(x)|\,dx.
\]
Taking the limit as $n \to \infty$ now yields the desired result.

\textbf{Third solution:} (by David Savitt)
We give an argument which yields the following improved result.  Let
$\mu_p$ and $\mu_n$ be the measure of the sets $\{ x \ : \ f(x) > 0\}$ and
$\{ x \ : \ f(x) < 0\}$ respectively, and let $\mu \le 1/2$ be
$\min(\mu_p,\mu_n)$.  Then
\begin{align*}
& \int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{1} |f(x) + f(y)|\,dx\,dy \\
&\ge (1 + (1-2\mu)^2)
\int_{0}^{1} |f(x)|\,dx.
\end{align*}
Note that the constant can be seen to be best possible by considering a
sequence of functions tending towards the step function which is $1$ on
$[0,\mu]$ and $-1$ on $(\mu,1]$.

Suppose without loss of generality that $\mu = \mu_p$.  As in the second
solution, it suffices to prove a
strengthened discrete analogue, namely
\[
\frac{1}{n^2} \sum_{i,j} |a_i + a_j| \ge
    \left(1 + \left(1 - \frac{2p}{n}\right)^2\right)\left(\frac{1}{n}
    \sum_{i=1}^{n} |a_i| \right),
\]
where $p \le n/2$ is the number of $a_1,\ldots,a_n$ which are positive.
(We need only make sure to choose meshes so that $p/n \to \mu$ as
$n \to \infty$.)
An equivalent inequality is
\[
\sum_{1 \le i < j \le n} | a_i + a_j | \ge \left(n - 1 - 2p +
    \frac{2p^2}{n}\right) \sum_{i=1}^{n} | a_i |.
\]

Write $r_i = |a_i|$, and assume without loss of generality that $r_i \ge
r_{i+1}$ for each $i$. Then for $i<j$,
$|a_i + a_j| = r_i + r_j$ if $a_i$ and $a_j$
have the same sign, and is $r_i - r_j$ if they have opposite signs. The
left-hand side is therefore equal to
\[
\sum_{i = 1}^n (n - i) r_i + \sum_{j = 1}^n r_j C_j,
\]
where
\begin{multline*}
C_j = \#\{ i < j \ : \sgn(a_i) = \sgn(a_j)\} \\
- \#\{ i < j : \sgn(a_i) \neq \sgn(a_j)\}.
\end{multline*}

Consider the partial sum $P_k = \sum_{j = 1}^{k} C_j$.  If exactly $p_k$ of
$a_1,\ldots,a_k$ are positive, then this sum is equal to
\[
\binom{p_k}{2} + \binom{k-p_k}{2} - \left[ \binom {k}{2} -
\binom{p_k}{2} - \binom{k-p_k}{2} \right],
\]
which expands and simplifies to
\[
-2 p_k (k-p_k) + \binom{k}{2} \,.
\]
For $k \le 2p$ even, this partial sum would be minimized with $p_k = \frac{k}{2}$,
and would then equal $-\frac{k}{2}$; for $k < 2p$ odd, this partial sum would
be minimized with $p_k = \frac{k \pm 1}{2}$, and would then equal
$-\frac{k-1}{2}$.  Either way, $P_k \ge - \lfloor \frac{k}{2} \rfloor$.  On the other
hand, if $k > 2p$, then
\[
-2 p_k (k-p_k) + \binom{k}{2} \ge -2 p(k-p) + \binom{k}{2}
\]
since $p_k$ is at most $p$. Define $Q_k$ to
be $- \lfloor \frac{k}{2} \rfloor$ if $k \le
2p$ and $-2 p(k-p) + \binom{k}{2}$ if $k \ge 2p$, so that $P_k \ge Q_k$.  Note
that $Q_1=0$.

Partial summation gives
\begin{align*}
\sum_{j = 1}^n r_j C_j & =  r_n P_n + \sum_{j=2}^{n} (r_{j-1} - r_j)
P_{j-1} \\
& \ge r_n Q_n + \sum_{j=2}^{n} (r_{j-1} - r_j) Q_{j-1} \\
& =  \sum_{j=2}^{n} r_j (Q_j - Q_{j-1}) \\
& =  - r_2 - r_4 - \cdots - r_{2p} + \sum_{j = 2p+1}^{n} (j-1-2p) r_j.
\end{align*}
It follows that
\begin{align*}
\sum_{1 \le i < j \le n} | a_i + a_j | 
    &= \sum_{i = 1}^n (n - i) r_i + \sum_{j = 1}^n r_j C_j \\
& \ge \sum_{i = 1}^{2p} (n - i - [ i \ \text{even}]) r_i \\
&\quad + \sum_{i = 2p+1}^{n} (n - 1 - 2p) r_i \\
&  = (n-1-2p) \sum_{i=1}^{n} r_i \\
&\quad + \sum_{i=1}^{2p} (2p + 1 - i - [i \ \text{even}]) r_i \\
&  \ge  (n-1-2p) \sum_{i=1}^{n} r_i + p \sum_{i=1}^{2p} r_i \\
&  \ge  (n-1-2p) \sum_{i=1}^{n} r_i + p\frac{2p}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} r_i\,,
\end{align*}
as desired.  The next-to-last and last inequalities each follow from the
monotonicity of the $r_i$'s, the former by pairing the $i^{\textrm{th}}$
term with the $(2p+1-i)^{\textrm{th}}$.

\textbf{Note:}
Compare the closely related Problem 6 from the 2000 USA Mathematical
Olympiad: prove that
for any nonnegative real numbers $a_1, \dots, a_n, b_1, \dots, b_n$,
one has
\[
\sum_{i,j=1}^n \min\{a_i a_j,b_i b_j\} \leq
\sum_{i,j=1}^n \min\{a_i b_j,a_j b_i\}.
\]

\end{itemize}

\end{document}

descriptive_long.question:
Let  $inputfunc(samplevariable)$  be  a continuous real-valued function defined on the interval
$[0,1]$. Show that
\[
   \int_0^1 \int_0^1 | inputfunc(samplevariable) + inputfunc(sampleother) |\,d samplevariable\,d sampleother \geq \int_0^1 |inputfunc(samplevariable)|\,d samplevariable.
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
\textbf{First solution:} (composite of solutions by Feng Xie and David
Pritchard)
Let $lebesguemeas$ denote Lebesgue measure on $[0,1]$. Define
\begin{align*}
possetdomain &= \{samplevariable \in [0,1]: inputfunc(samplevariable) \geq 0\} \\
negsetdomain &= \{samplevariable \in [0,1]: inputfunc(samplevariable) < 0\};
\end{align*}
then $possetdomain$, $negsetdomain$ are measurable and $lebesguemeas(possetdomain) + lebesguemeas(negsetdomain) = 1$.
Write $posmeasure$ and $negmeasure$ for $lebesguemeas(possetdomain)$ and $lebesguemeas(negsetdomain)$.
Also define
\begin{align*}
posintegral &= \int_{possetdomain} |inputfunc(samplevariable)|\,d samplevariable \\
negintegral &= \int_{negsetdomain} |inputfunc(samplevariable)|\,d samplevariable,
\end{align*}
so that $\int_0^1 |inputfunc(samplevariable)|\,d samplevariable = posintegral + negintegral.

From the triangle inequality $|a+b| \geq \pm(|a| - |b|)$,
we have the inequality
\begin{align*}
&\iint_{possetdomain \times negsetdomain} |inputfunc(samplevariable) + inputfunc(sampleother)|\,d samplevariable\,d sampleother \\
&\geq
\pm \iint_{possetdomain \times negsetdomain} (|inputfunc(samplevariable)| - |inputfunc(sampleother)|)\,d samplevariable\,d sampleother \\
&= \pm ( negmeasure \, posintegral - posmeasure \, negintegral),
\end{align*}
and likewise with $+$ and $-$ switched. Adding these inequalities together
and allowing all possible choices of the signs, we get
\begin{align*}
&\iint_{(possetdomain \times negsetdomain) \cup (negsetdomain \times possetdomain)} |inputfunc(samplevariable) + inputfunc(sampleother)|\,d samplevariable\,d sampleother \\
&\geq
\max\left\{ 0, 2 (negmeasure \, posintegral - posmeasure \, negintegral), 2 (posmeasure \, negintegral - negmeasure \, posintegral) \right\}.
\end{align*}
To this inequality, we add the equalities
\begin{align*}
\iint_{possetdomain \times possetdomain} |inputfunc(samplevariable) + inputfunc(sampleother)|\,d samplevariable\,d sampleother &= 2 \, posmeasure \, posintegral \\
\iint_{negsetdomain \times negsetdomain} |inputfunc(samplevariable) + inputfunc(sampleother)|\,d samplevariable\,d sampleother &= 2 \, negmeasure \, negintegral \\
-\int_0^1 |inputfunc(samplevariable)|\,d samplevariable &= -(posmeasure + negmeasure)(posintegral + negintegral)
\end{align*}
to obtain
\begin{multline*}
\int_0^1 \int_0^1 |inputfunc(samplevariable)+inputfunc(sampleother)|\,d samplevariable\,d sampleother - \int_0^1 |inputfunc(samplevariable)|\,d samplevariable \\
\geq \max\{ (posmeasure - negmeasure)(posintegral + negintegral)+ 2\,negmeasure\,(negintegral - posintegral), \\
(posmeasure - negmeasure)(posintegral - negintegral), \\
(negmeasure - posmeasure)(posintegral + negintegral)+ 2\,posmeasure\,(posintegral - negintegral) \}.
\end{multline*}
Now simply note that for each of the possible comparisons between
posmeasure and negmeasure, and between posintegral and negintegral, one of the three
terms above is manifestly nonnegative. This yields the desired result.

\textbf{Second solution:}
We will show at the end that it
is enough to prove a discrete analogue: if samplevariable$_1, \dots, samplevariable$_{totalcount}$
are real numbers, then
\[
\frac{1}{totalcount^2} \sum_{indexfirst,indexsecond=1}^{totalcount} |vectorxi + vectorxj| \geq \frac{1}{totalcount}
\sum_{indexfirst=1}^{totalcount} |vectorxi|.
\]
In the meantime, we concentrate on this assertion.

Let $inputfunc(samplevariable_1, \dots, samplevariable_{totalcount})$ denote the difference between the two sides.
We induct
on the number of nonzero values of $|vectorxi|$. We leave for later the base case,
where there is at most one such value. Suppose instead for now that there
are two or more. Let smallestval be the smallest, and suppose without loss of
generality that samplevariable$_1 = \cdots = samplevariable_{countapositive} = smallestval$, samplevariable$_{countapositive+1} = \cdots = samplevariable_{countapositive+countnegative} = -smallestval$,
and for indexfirst > countapositive+countnegative, either samplevariable$_{indexfirst} = 0$ or $|samplevariable_{indexfirst}| > smallestval$. (One of countapositive,countnegative might be
zero.)

Now consider
\[
inputfunc(\overbrace{tempvalue, \cdots,tempvalue}^{\mbox{$countapositive$ terms}},\overbrace{-tempvalue,\cdots,-tempvalue}^{\mbox{$countnegative$ terms}},samplevariable_{countapositive+countnegative+1},\cdots, samplevariable_{totalcount})
\]
as a function of tempvalue.
It is piecewise linear near smallestval; in fact, it is
linear between 0 and
the smallest nonzero value among $|samplevariable_{countapositive+countnegative+1}|, \dots, |samplevariable_{totalcount}|$
(which exists by hypothesis). Thus its minimum is achieved by one (or both)
of those two endpoints. In other words, we can reduce the
number of distinct nonzero absolute values among the vectorxi without
increasing inputfunc. This
yields the induction, pending verification of the base case.

As for the base case, suppose that samplevariable$_1 = \cdots = samplevariable_{countapositive} = smallestval > 0$,
samplevariable$_{countapositive+1} = \cdots = samplevariable_{countapositive+countnegative} = -smallestval$, and samplevariable$_{countapositive+countnegative+1} = \cdots = samplevariable_{totalcount} = 0$.
(Here one or even both of countapositive,countnegative could be zero, though the latter case
is trivial.)
Then
\begin{multline*}
inputfunc(samplevariable_1, \dots, samplevariable_{totalcount}) = \frac{smallestval}{totalcount^2} (2countapositive^2 + 2countnegative^2 + (countapositive+countnegative)(totalcount-countapositive-countnegative)) \\
- \frac{smallestval}{totalcount} (countapositive+countnegative) = \frac{smallestval}{totalcount^2} (countapositive^2 -2countapositive countnegative + countnegative^2) \geq 0.
\end{multline*}
This proves the base case of the induction, completing the solution
of the discrete analogue.

To deduce the original statement from the discrete analogue,
approximate both integrals
by equally-spaced Riemann sums and take limits. This works because
given a continuous function
on a product of closed intervals,
any sequence of Riemann sums with mesh size tending to zero
converges to the integral. (The domain is compact, so
the function is uniformly continuous. Hence for any $\epsilon > 0$
there is a cutoff below
which any mesh size forces the
discrepancy between the Riemann sum and the integral to be less than
$\epsilon$.)

Alternate derivation (based on a solution by Dan Bernstein):
from the discrete analogue, we have
\[
\sum_{1 \leq indexfirst<indexsecond\leq totalcount} |inputfunc(vectorxi) + inputfunc(vectorxj)| \geq \frac{totalcount-2}{2}
\sum_{indexfirst=1}^{totalcount} |inputfunc(vectorxi)|,
\]
for all samplevariable$_1, \dots, samplevariable$_{totalcount} \in [0,1]$. Integrating both sides as
$(samplevariable_1, \dots, samplevariable_{totalcount})$ runs over
$[0,1]^{totalcount}$ yields
\begin{align*}
&\frac{totalcount(totalcount-1)}{2} \int_0^1 \int_0^1 |inputfunc(samplevariable)+inputfunc(sampleother)|\,d sampleother\,d samplevariable \\
&\geq
\frac{totalcount(totalcount-2)}{2} \int_0^1 |inputfunc(samplevariable)|\,d samplevariable,
\end{align*}
or
\[
\int_0^1 \int_0^1 |inputfunc(samplevariable)+inputfunc(sampleother)|\,d sampleother\,d samplevariable
\geq
\frac{totalcount-2}{totalcount-1} \int_0^1 |inputfunc(samplevariable)|\,d samplevariable.
\]
Taking the limit as totalcount \to \infty now yields the desired result.

\textbf{Third solution:} (by David Savitt)
We give an argument which yields the following improved result.  Let
posmeasure$_p$ and negmeasure$_n$ be the measure of the sets $\{ samplevariable  \ : \ inputfunc(samplevariable) > 0\}$ and
$\{ samplevariable  \ : \ inputfunc(samplevariable) < 0\}$ respectively, and let lebesguemeas $\le 1/2$ be
$\min(posmeasure$_p$,negmeasure$_n$)$.  Then
\begin{align*}
& \int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{1} |inputfunc(samplevariable) + inputfunc(sampleother)|\,d samplevariable\,d sampleother \\
&\ge (1 + (1-2lebesguemeas)^2)
\int_{0}^{1} |inputfunc(samplevariable)|\,d samplevariable.
\end{align*}
Note that the constant can be seen to be best possible by considering a
sequence of functions tending towards the step function which is $1$ on
$[0,lebesguemeas]$ and $-1$ on $(lebesguemeas,1]$.

Suppose without loss of generality that lebesguemeas = posmeasure$_p$.  As in the second
solution, it suffices to prove a
strengthened discrete analogue, namely
\[
\frac{1}{totalcount^2} \sum_{indexfirst,indexsecond} |countapositive_{indexfirst} + countapositive_{indexsecond}| \ge
    \left(1 + \left(1 - \frac{2positivecnt}{totalcount}\right)^2\right)\left(\frac{1}{totalcount}
    \sum_{indexfirst=1}^{totalcount} |countapositive_{indexfirst}| \right),
\]
where positivecnt \le totalcount/2 is the number of countapositive$_1,\ldots,countapositive_{totalcount}$ which are positive.
(We need only make sure to choose meshes so that positivecnt/totalcount \to lebesguemeas as
totalcount \to \infty$.)
An equivalent inequality is
\[
\sum_{1 \le indexfirst < indexsecond \le totalcount} | countapositive_{indexfirst} + countapositive_{indexsecond} | \ge \left(totalcount - 1 - 2positivecnt +
    \frac{2positivecnt^2}{totalcount}\right) \sum_{indexfirst=1}^{totalcount} | countapositive_{indexfirst} |.
\]

Write $r_{indexfirst} = |countapositive_{indexfirst}|$, and assume without loss of generality that $r_{indexfirst} \ge
r_{indexfirst+1}$ for each indexfirst. Then for indexfirst<indexsecond,
$|countapositive_{indexfirst} + countapositive_{indexsecond}| = r_{indexfirst} + r_{indexsecond}$ if countapositive_{indexfirst} and countapositive_{indexsecond}
have the same sign, and is $r_{indexfirst} - r_{indexsecond}$ if they have opposite signs. The
left-hand side is therefore equal to
\[
\sum_{indexfirst = 1}^{totalcount} (totalcount - indexfirst) r_{indexfirst} + \sum_{indexsecond = 1}^{totalcount} r_{indexsecond} C_{indexsecond},
\]
where
\begin{multline*}
C_{indexsecond} = \#\{ indexfirst < indexsecond \ : \sgn(countapositive_{indexfirst}) = \sgn(countapositive_{indexsecond})\} \\
- \#\{ indexfirst < indexsecond : \sgn(countapositive_{indexfirst}) \neq \sgn(countapositive_{indexsecond})\}.
\end{multline*}

Consider the partial sum partialpk = \sum_{indexsecond = 1}^{indexthird} C_{indexsecond}.  If exactly posuntilk of
countapositive$_1,\ldots,countapositive_{indexthird}$ are positive, then this sum is equal to
\[
\binom{posuntilk}{2} + \binom{indexthird-posuntilk}{2} - \left[ \binom {indexthird}{2} -
\binom{posuntilk}{2} - \binom{indexthird-posuntilk}{2} \right],
\]
which expands and simplifies to
\[
-2 posuntilk (indexthird-posuntilk) + \binom{indexthird}{2} \,.
\]
For indexthird \le 2positivecnt even, this partial sum would be minimized with posuntilk = \frac{indexthird}{2}$,
and would then equal $-\frac{indexthird}{2}$; for indexthird < 2positivecnt odd, this partial sum would
be minimized with posuntilk = \frac{indexthird \pm 1}{2}$, and would then equal
$-\frac{indexthird-1}{2}$.  Either way, partialpk \ge - \lfloor \frac{indexthird}{2} \rfloor$.  On the other
hand, if indexthird > 2positivecnt, then
\[
-2 posuntilk (indexthird-posuntilk) + \binom{indexthird}{2} \ge -2 positivecnt(indexthird-positivecnt) + \binom{indexthird}{2}
\]
since posuntilk is at most positivecnt. Define boundqk to
be $- \lfloor \frac{indexthird}{2} \rfloor$ if indexthird \le
2positivecnt and $-2 positivecnt(indexthird-positivecnt) + \binom{indexthird}{2}$ if indexthird \ge 2positivecnt, so that partialpk \ge boundqk$.  Note
that boundqk$_1=0$.

Partial summation gives
\begin{align*}
\sum_{indexsecond = 1}^{totalcount} r_{indexsecond} C_{indexsecond} & =  r_{totalcount} partialpk + \sum_{indexsecond=2}^{totalcount} (r_{indexsecond-1} - r_{indexsecond})
boundqk \\
& \ge r_{totalcount} boundqk + \sum_{indexsecond=2}^{totalcount} (r_{indexsecond-1} - r_{indexsecond}) boundqk \\
& =  \sum_{indexsecond=2}^{totalcount} r_{indexsecond} (boundqk - boundqk) \\
& =  - r_2 - r_4 - \cdots - r_{2positivecnt} + \sum_{indexsecond = 2positivecnt+1}^{totalcount} (indexsecond-1-2positivecnt) r_{indexsecond}.
\end{align*}
It follows that
\begin{align*}
\sum_{1 \le indexfirst < indexsecond \le totalcount} | countapositive_{indexfirst} + countapositive_{indexsecond} | 
    &= \sum_{indexfirst = 1}^{totalcount} (totalcount - indexfirst) r_{indexfirst} + \sum_{indexsecond = 1}^{totalcount} r_{indexsecond} C_{indexsecond} \\
& \ge \sum_{indexfirst = 1}^{2positivecnt} (totalcount - indexfirst - [ indexfirst \ \text{even}]) r_{indexfirst} \\
&\quad + \sum_{indexfirst = 2positivecnt+1}^{totalcount} (totalcount - 1 - 2positivecnt) r_{indexfirst} \\
&  = (totalcount-1-2positivecnt) \sum_{indexfirst=1}^{totalcount} r_{indexfirst} \\
&\quad + \sum_{indexfirst=1}^{2positivecnt} (2positivecnt + 1 - indexfirst - [indexfirst \ \text{even}]) r_{indexfirst} \\
&  \ge  (totalcount-1-2positivecnt) \sum_{indexfirst=1}^{totalcount} r_{indexfirst} + positivecnt \sum_{indexfirst=1}^{2positivecnt} r_{indexfirst} \\
&  \ge  (totalcount-1-2positivecnt) \sum_{indexfirst=1}^{totalcount} r_{indexfirst} + positivecnt\frac{2positivecnt}{totalcount} \sum_{indexfirst=1}^{totalcount} r_{indexfirst}\,,
\end{align*}
as desired.  The next-to-last and last inequalities each follow from the
monotonicity of the $r_{indexfirst}$'s, the former by pairing the $indexfirst^{\textrm{th}}$
term with the $(2positivecnt+1-indexfirst)^{\textrm{th}}$.

\textbf{Note:}
Compare the closely related Problem 6 from the 2000 USA Mathematical
Olympiad: prove that
for any nonnegative real numbers countapositive$_1, \dots, countapositive_{totalcount}, b_1, \dots, b_{totalcount}$,
one has
\[
\sum_{indexfirst,indexsecond=1}^{totalcount} \min\{countapositive_{indexfirst} countapositive_{indexsecond},b_{indexfirst} b_{indexsecond}\} \leq
\sum_{indexfirst,indexsecond=1}^{totalcount} \min\{countapositive_{indexfirst} b_{indexsecond},countapositive_{indexsecond} b_{indexfirst}\}.
\]

\end{itemize}

\end{document}

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let  $pineapple(waterfall)$  be  a continuous real-valued function defined on the interval
$[0,1]$. Show that
\[
   \int_0^1 \int_0^1 | pineapple(waterfall) + pineapple(mountains) |\,dwaterfall\,dmountains \geq \int_0^1 |pineapple(waterfall)|\,dwaterfall.
\]

\end{itemize}
\end{document}
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
\textbf{First solution:} (composite of solutions by Feng Xie and David
Pritchard)
Let $cylinder$ denote Lebesgue measure on $[0,1]$. Define
\begin{align*}
rainbow &= \{waterfall \in [0,1]: pineapple(waterfall) \geq 0\} \\
sunshine &= \{waterfall \in [0,1]: pineapple(waterfall) < 0\};
\end{align*}
then rainbow, sunshine are measurable and $cylinder(rainbow) + cylinder(sunshine) = 1$.
Write volcano and glacier for $cylinder(rainbow)$ and $cylinder(sunshine)$.
Also define
\begin{align*}
meadowland &= \int_{rainbow} |pineapple(waterfall)|\,dwaterfall \\
riverbank &= \int_{sunshine} |pineapple(waterfall)|\,dwaterfall,
\end{align*}
so that $\int_0^1 |pineapple(waterfall)|\,dwaterfall = meadowland + riverbank$.

From the triangle inequality $|a+b| \geq \pm(|a| - |b|)$,
we have the inequality
\begin{align*}
&\iint_{rainbow \times sunshine} |pineapple(waterfall) + pineapple(mountains)|\,dwaterfall\,dmountains \\
&\geq
\pm \iint_{rainbow \times sunshine} (|pineapple(waterfall)| - |pineapple(mountains)|)\,dwaterfall\,dmountains \\
&= \pm ( glacier\, meadowland - volcano\, riverbank),
\end{align*}
and likewise with $+$ and $-$ switched. Adding these inequalities together
and allowing all possible choices of the signs, we get
\begin{align*}
&\iint_{(rainbow \times sunshine) \cup (sunshine \times rainbow)} |pineapple(waterfall) + pineapple(mountains)|\,dwaterfall\,dmountains \\
&\geq
\max\left\{ 0, 2 (glacier\, meadowland - volcano\, riverbank), 2 (volcano\, riverbank - glacier\, meadowland) \right\}.
\end{align*}
To this inequality, we add the equalities
\begin{align*}
\iint_{rainbow \times rainbow} |pineapple(waterfall) + pineapple(mountains)|\,dwaterfall\,dmountains &= 2\,volcano\,meadowland \\
\iint_{sunshine \times sunshine} |pineapple(waterfall) + pineapple(mountains)|\,dwaterfall\,dmountains &= 2\,glacier\,riverbank \\
-\int_0^1 |pineapple(waterfall)|\,dwaterfall &= -(volcano + glacier)(meadowland + riverbank)
\end{align*}
to obtain
\begin{multline*}
\int_0^1 \int_0^1 |pineapple(waterfall)+pineapple(mountains)|\,dwaterfall\,dmountains - \int_0^1 |pineapple(waterfall)|\,dwaterfall \\
\geq \max\{ (volcano - glacier)(meadowland + riverbank)+ 2\,glacier\,(riverbank - meadowland), \\
(volcano - glacier)(meadowland - riverbank), \\
(glacier - volcano)(meadowland + riverbank)+ 2\,volcano\,(meadowland - riverbank) \}.
\end{multline*}
Now simply note that for each of the possible comparisons between
volcano and glacier, and between meadowland and riverbank, one of the three
terms above is manifestly nonnegative. This yields the desired result.

\textbf{Second solution:}
We will show at the end that it
is enough to prove a discrete analogue: if $waterfall_1, \dots, waterfall_{strawberry}$
are real numbers, then
\[
\frac{1}{strawberry^2} \sum_{\substack{raincloud,butterfly=1}}^{strawberry} |moonlight + starlight| \geq \frac{1}{strawberry}
\sum_{raincloud=1}^{strawberry} |moonlight|.
\]
In the meantime, we concentrate on this assertion.

Let $pineapple(waterfall_1, \dots, waterfall_{strawberry})$ denote the difference between the two sides.
We induct
on the number of nonzero values of $|moonlight|$. We leave for later the base case,
where there is at most one such value. Suppose instead for now that there
are two or more. Let sandstorm be the smallest, and suppose without loss of
generality that $waterfall_1 = \cdots = waterfall_{honeycomb} = sandstorm$, $waterfall_{honeycomb+1} = \cdots = waterfall_{honeycomb+dragonfly} = -sandstorm$,
and for $raincloud > honeycomb+dragonfly$, either $waterfall_{raincloud} = 0$ or $|waterfall_{raincloud}| > sandstorm$. (One of honeycomb,dragonfly might be
zero.)

Now consider
\[
pineapple(\overbrace{whirlpool, \cdots,whirlpool}^{\mbox{$honeycomb$ terms}},\overbrace{-whirlpool,\cdots,-whirlpool}^{\mbox{$dragonfly$ terms}},waterfall_{honeycomb+dragonfly+1},\cdots, waterfall_{strawberry})
\]
as a function of whirlpool.
It is piecewise linear near sandstorm; in fact, it is
linear between 0 and
the smallest nonzero value among $|waterfall_{honeycomb+dragonfly+1}|, \dots, |waterfall_{strawberry}|$
(which exists by hypothesis). Thus its minimum is achieved by one (or both)
of those two endpoints. In other words, we can reduce the
number of distinct nonzero absolute values among the $waterfall_{raincloud}$ without
increasing pineapple. This
yields the induction, pending verification of the base case.

As for the base case, suppose that $waterfall_1 = \cdots = waterfall_{honeycomb} = sandstorm > 0$,
$waterfall_{honeycomb+1} = \cdots = waterfall_{honeycomb+dragonfly} = -sandstorm$, and $waterfall_{honeycomb+dragonfly+1} = \cdots = waterfall_{strawberry} = 0$.
(Here one or even both of honeycomb,dragonfly could be zero, though the latter case
is trivial.)
Then
\begin{multline*}
pineapple(waterfall_1, \dots, waterfall_{strawberry}) = \frac{sandstorm}{strawberry^2} (2honeycomb^2 + 2dragonfly^2 + (honeycomb+dragonfly)(strawberry-honeycomb-dragonfly)) \\
- \frac{sandstorm}{strawberry} (honeycomb+dragonfly) = \frac{sandstorm}{strawberry^2} (honeycomb^2 -2honeycomb\,dragonfly + dragonfly^2) \geq 0.
\end{multline*}
This proves the base case of the induction, completing the solution
of the discrete analogue.

To deduce the original statement from the discrete analogue,
approximate both integrals
by equally-spaced Riemann sums and take limits. This works because
given a continuous function
on a product of closed intervals,
any sequence of Riemann sums with mesh size tending to zero
converges to the integral. (The domain is compact, so
the function is uniformly continuous. Hence for any $\epsilon > 0$
there is a cutoff below
which any mesh size forces the
discrepancy between the Riemann sum and the integral to be less than
$\epsilon$.)

Alternate derivation (based on a solution by Dan Bernstein):
from the discrete analogue, we have
\[
\sum_{1 \leq raincloud<butterfly\leq strawberry} |pineapple(waterfall_{raincloud}) + pineapple(waterfall_{butterfly})| \geq \frac{strawberry-2}{2}
\sum_{raincloud=1}^{strawberry} |pineapple(waterfall_{raincloud})|,
\]
for all $waterfall_1, \dots, waterfall_{strawberry} \in [0,1]$. Integrating both sides as
$(waterfall_1, \dots, waterfall_{strawberry})$ runs over
$[0,1]^{strawberry}$ yields
\begin{align*}
&\frac{strawberry(strawberry-1)}{2} \int_0^1 \int_0^1 |pineapple(waterfall)+pineapple(mountains)|\,dmountains\,dwaterfall \\
&\geq
\frac{strawberry(strawberry-2)}{2} \int_0^1 |pineapple(waterfall)|\,dwaterfall,
\end{align*}
or
\[
\int_0^1 \int_0^1 |pineapple(waterfall)+pineapple(mountains)|\,dmountains\,dwaterfall
\geq
\frac{strawberry-2}{strawberry-1} \int_0^1 |pineapple(waterfall)|\,dwaterfall.
\]
Taking the limit as strawberry $\to \infty$ now yields the desired result.

\textbf{Third solution:} (by David Savitt)
We give an argument which yields the following improved result.  Let
cylinder_salamander and cylinder_strawberry be the measure of the sets $\{ waterfall \ : \ pineapple(waterfall) > 0\}$ and
$\{ waterfall \ : \ pineapple(waterfall) < 0\}$ respectively, and let cylinder \le 1/2 be
$\min(cylinder_salamander,cylinder_strawberry)$.  Then
\begin{align*}
& \int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{1} |pineapple(waterfall) + pineapple(mountains)|\,dwaterfall\,dmountains \\
&\ge (1 + (1-2cylinder)^2)
\int_{0}^{1} |pineapple(waterfall)|\,dwaterfall.
\end{align*}
Note that the constant can be seen to be best possible by considering a
sequence of functions tending towards the step function which is $1$ on
$[0,cylinder]$ and $-1$ on $(cylinder,1]$.

Suppose without loss of generality that cylinder = cylinder_salamander.  As in the second
solution, it suffices to prove a
strengthened discrete analogue, namely
\[
\frac{1}{strawberry^2} \sum_{raincloud,butterfly} |a_{raincloud} + a_{butterfly}| \ge
    \left(1 + \left(1 - \frac{2salamander}{strawberry}\right)^2\right)\left(\frac{1}{strawberry}
    \sum_{raincloud=1}^{strawberry} |a_{raincloud}| \right),
\]
where salamander \le strawberry/2 is the number of $a_1,\ldots,a_{strawberry}$ which are positive.
(We need only make sure to choose meshes so that salamander/strawberry \to cylinder as
strawberry \to \infty.)
An equivalent inequality is
\[
\sum_{1 \le raincloud < butterfly \le strawberry} | a_{raincloud} + a_{butterfly} | \ge \left(strawberry - 1 - 2salamander +
    \frac{2salamander^2}{strawberry}\right) \sum_{raincloud=1}^{strawberry} | a_{raincloud} |.
\]

Write caterpillar = |a_{raincloud}|, and assume without loss of generality that caterpillar \ge
caterpillar_{raincloud+1} for each raincloud. Then for raincloud<butterfly,
$|a_{raincloud} + a_{butterfly}| = caterpillar + caterpillar_{butterfly}$ if $a_{raincloud}$ and $a_{butterfly}$
have the same sign, and is $caterpillar - caterpillar_{butterfly}$ if they have opposite signs. The
left-hand side is therefore equal to
\[
\sum_{raincloud = 1}^{strawberry} (strawberry - raincloud) caterpillar + \sum_{butterfly = 1}^{strawberry} caterpillar_{butterfly} C_{butterfly},
\]
where
\begin{multline*}
C_{butterfly} = \#\{ raincloud < butterfly \ : \sgn(a_{raincloud}) = \sgn(a_{butterfly})\} \\
- \#\{ raincloud < butterfly : \sgn(a_{raincloud}) \neq \sgn(a_{butterfly})\}.
\end{multline*}

Consider the partial sum rainforest_{seashore} = \sum_{butterfly = 1}^{seashore} C_{butterfly}.  If exactly peppermint of
$a_1,\ldots,a_{seashore}$ are positive, then this sum is equal to
\[
\binom{peppermint}{2} + \binom{seashore-peppermint}{2} - \left[ \binom {seashore}{2} -
\binom{peppermint}{2} - \binom{seashore-peppermint}{2} \right],
\]
which expands and simplifies to
\[
-2 peppermint (seashore-peppermint) + \binom{seashore}{2} \,.
\]
For seashore \le 2salamander even, this partial sum would be minimized with peppermint = \frac{seashore}{2},
and would then equal -\frac{seashore}{2}; for seashore < 2salamander odd, this partial sum would
be minimized with peppermint = \frac{seashore \pm 1}{2}, and would then equal
-\frac{seashore-1}{2}.  Either way, rainforest_{seashore} \ge - \lfloor \frac{seashore}{2} \rfloor.  On the other
hand, if seashore > 2salamander, then
\[
-2 peppermint (seashore-peppermint) + \binom{seashore}{2} \ge -2 salamander(seashore-salamander) + \binom{seashore}{2}
\]
since peppermint is at most salamander. Define canterbury_{seashore} to
be - \lfloor \frac{seashore}{2} \rfloor if seashore \le
2salamander and -2 salamander(seashore-salamander) + \binom{seashore}{2} if seashore \ge 2salamander, so that rainforest_{seashore} \ge canterbury_{seashore}.  Note
that canterbury_1=0.

Partial summation gives
\begin{align*}
\sum_{butterfly = 1}^{strawberry} caterpillar_{butterfly} C_{butterfly} & =  caterpillar_{strawberry} rainforest_{strawberry} + \sum_{butterfly=2}^{strawberry} (caterpillar_{butterfly-1} - caterpillar_{butterfly})
rainforest_{butterfly-1} \\
& \ge caterpillar_{strawberry} canterbury_{strawberry} + \sum_{butterfly=2}^{strawberry} (caterpillar_{butterfly-1} - caterpillar_{butterfly}) canterbury_{butterfly-1} \\
& =  \sum_{butterfly=2}^{strawberry} caterpillar_{butterfly} (canterbury_{butterfly} - canterbury_{butterfly-1}) \\
& =  - caterpillar_2 - caterpillar_4 - \cdots - caterpillar_{2salamander} + \sum_{butterfly = 2salamander+1}^{strawberry} (butterfly-1-2salamander) caterpillar_{butterfly}.
\end{align*}
It follows that
\begin{align*}
\sum_{1 \le raincloud < butterfly \le strawberry} | a_{raincloud} + a_{butterfly} | 
    &= \sum_{raincloud = 1}^{strawberry} (strawberry - raincloud) caterpillar + \sum_{butterfly = 1}^{strawberry} caterpillar_{butterfly} C_{butterfly} \\
& \ge \sum_{raincloud = 1}^{2salamander} (strawberry - raincloud - [ raincloud \ \text{even}]) caterpillar \\
&\quad + \sum_{raincloud = 2salamander+1}^{strawberry} (strawberry - 1 - 2salamander) caterpillar \\
&  = (strawberry-1-2salamander) \sum_{raincloud=1}^{strawberry} caterpillar \\
&\quad + \sum_{raincloud=1}^{2salamander} (2salamander + 1 - raincloud - [raincloud \ \text{even}]) caterpillar \\
&  \ge  (strawberry-1-2salamander) \sum_{raincloud=1}^{strawberry} caterpillar + salamander \sum_{raincloud=1}^{2salamander} caterpillar \\
&  \ge  (strawberry-1-2salamander) \sum_{raincloud=1}^{strawberry} caterpillar + salamander\frac{2salamander}{strawberry} \sum_{raincloud=1}^{strawberry} caterpillar\,,
\end{align*}
as desired.  The next-to-last and last inequalities each follow from the
monotonicity of the caterpillar's, the former by pairing the raincloud^{\textrm{th}}
term with the $(2salamander+1-raincloud)^{\textrm{th}}$.

\textbf{Note:}
Compare the closely related Problem 6 from the 2000 USA Mathematical
Olympiad: prove that
for any nonnegative real numbers $a_1, \dots, a_{strawberry}, b_1, \dots, b_{strawberry}$,
one has
\[
\sum_{raincloud,butterfly=1}^{strawberry} \min\{a_{raincloud} a_{butterfly},b_{raincloud} b_{butterfly}\} \leq
\sum_{raincloud,butterfly=1}^{strawberry} \min\{a_{raincloud} b_{butterfly},a_{butterfly} b_{raincloud}\}.
\]

\end{itemize}

\end{document}

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let  $constantmap(dependentvar)$  be  a continuous real-valued function defined on the interval
$[0,1]$. Show that
\[
   \int_0^1 \int_0^1 | constantmap(dependentvar) + constantmap(constantvar) |\,ddependentvar\,dconstantvar \geq \int_0^1 |constantmap(dependentvar)|\,ddependentvar.
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
\textbf{First solution:} (composite of solutions by Feng Xie and David
Pritchard)
Let $vacuumweight$ denote Lebesgue measure on $[0,1]$. Define
\begin{align*}
negativeset &= \{dependentvar \in [0,1]: constantmap(dependentvar) \geq 0\} \\
positiveset &= \{dependentvar \in [0,1]: constantmap(dependentvar) < 0\};
\end{align*}
then negativeset, positiveset are measurable and $vacuumweight(negativeset) + vacuumweight(positiveset) = 1$.
Write negativemeasure and positivemeasure for $vacuumweight(negativeset)$ and $vacuumweight(positiveset)$.
Also define
\begin{align*}
negativeintegral &= \int_{negativeset} |constantmap(dependentvar)|\,ddependentvar \\
positiveintegral &= \int_{positiveset} |constantmap(dependentvar)|\,ddependentvar,
\end{align*}
so that $\int_0^1 |constantmap(dependentvar)|\,ddependentvar = negativeintegral + positiveintegral.

From the triangle inequality $|a+b| \geq \pm(|a| - |b|)$,
we have the inequality
\begin{align*}
&\iint_{negativeset \times positiveset} |constantmap(dependentvar) + constantmap(constantvar)|\,ddependentvar\,dconstantvar \\
&\geq
\pm \iint_{negativeset \times positiveset} (|constantmap(dependentvar)| - |constantmap(constantvar)|)\,ddependentvar\,dconstantvar \\
&= \pm ( positivemeasure\, negativeintegral - negativemeasure\, positiveintegral),
\end{align*}
and likewise with $+$ and $-$ switched. Adding these inequalities together
and allowing all possible choices of the signs, we get
\begin{align*}
&\iint_{(negativeset \times positiveset) \cup (positiveset \times negativeset)} |constantmap(dependentvar) + constantmap(constantvar)|\,ddependentvar\,dconstantvar \\
&\geq
\max\left\{ 0, 2 (positivemeasure\, negativeintegral - negativemeasure\, positiveintegral), 2 (negativemeasure\, positiveintegral - positivemeasure\, negativeintegral) \right\}.
\end{align*}
To this inequality, we add the equalities
\begin{align*}
\iint_{negativeset \times negativeset} |constantmap(dependentvar) + constantmap(constantvar)|\,ddependentvar\,dconstantvar &= 2 negativemeasure\, negativeintegral \\
\iint_{positiveset \times positiveset} |constantmap(dependentvar) + constantmap(constantvar)|\,ddependentvar\,dconstantvar &= 2 positivemeasure\, positiveintegral \\
-\int_0^1 |constantmap(dependentvar)|\,ddependentvar &= -(negativemeasure + positivemeasure)(negativeintegral + positiveintegral)
\end{align*}
to obtain
\begin{multline*}
\int_0^1 \int_0^1 |constantmap(dependentvar)+constantmap(constantvar)|\,ddependentvar\,dconstantvar - \int_0^1 |constantmap(dependentvar)|\,ddependentvar \\
\geq \max\{ (negativemeasure - positivemeasure)(negativeintegral + positiveintegral)+ 2positivemeasure(positiveintegral - negativeintegral), \\
(negativemeasure - positivemeasure)(negativeintegral - positiveintegral), \\
(positivemeasure - negativemeasure)(negativeintegral + positiveintegral)+ 2negativemeasure(negativeintegral - positiveintegral) \}.
\end{multline*}
Now simply note that for each of the possible comparisons between
negativemeasure and positivemeasure, and between negativeintegral and positiveintegral, one of the three
terms above is manifestly nonnegative. This yields the desired result.

\textbf{Second solution:}
We will show at the end that it
is enough to prove a discrete analogue: if $dependentvar_1, \dots, dependentvar_{continuity}$
are real numbers, then
\[
\frac{1}{continuity^2} \sum_{terminalindex,startingindex=1}^{continuity} |dependententry + constantentry| \geq \frac{1}{continuity}
\sum_{terminalindex=1}^{continuity} |dependententry|.
\]
In the meantime, we concentrate on this assertion.

Let $constantmap(dependentvar_1, \dots, dependentvar_{continuity})$ denote the difference between the two sides.
We induct
on the number of nonzero values of $|dependententry|$. We leave for later the base case,
where there is at most one such value. Suppose instead for now that there
are two or more. Let largestvalue be the smallest, and suppose without loss of
generality that $dependentvar_1 = \cdots = dependentvar_{maxcount} = largestvalue$, $dependentvar_{maxcount+1} = \cdots = dependentvar_{maxcount+mincount} = -largestvalue$,
and for $terminalindex > maxcount+mincount$, either $dependententry = 0$ or $|dependententry| > largestvalue$. (One of $maxcount,mincount$ might be
zero.)

Now consider
\[
constantmap(\overbrace{steadyvalue, \cdots,steadyvalue}^{\mbox{$maxcount$ terms}},\overbrace{-steadyvalue,\cdots,-steadyvalue}^{\mbox{$mincount$ terms}},dependentvar_{maxcount+mincount+1},\cdots, dependentvar_{continuity})
\]
as a function of steadyvalue.
It is piecewise linear near largestvalue; in fact, it is
linear between 0 and
the smallest nonzero value among $|dependentvar_{maxcount+mincount+1}|, \dots, |dependentvar_{continuity}|$
(which exists by hypothesis). Thus its minimum is achieved by one (or both)
of those two endpoints. In other words, we can reduce the
number of distinct nonzero absolute values among the dependentvar without
increasing constantmap. This
yields the induction, pending verification of the base case.

As for the base case, suppose that $dependentvar_1 = \cdots = dependentvar_{maxcount} = largestvalue > 0$,
$dependentvar_{maxcount+1} = \cdots = dependentvar_{maxcount+mincount} = -largestvalue$, and $dependentvar_{maxcount+mincount+1} = \cdots = dependentvar_{continuity} = 0$.
(Here one or even both of $maxcount,mincount$ could be zero, though the latter case
is trivial.)
Then
\begin{multline*}
constantmap(dependentvar_1, \dots, dependentvar_{continuity}) = \frac{largestvalue}{continuity^2} (2maxcount^2 + 2mincount^2 + (maxcount+mincount)(continuity-maxcount-mincount)) \\
- \frac{largestvalue}{continuity} (maxcount+mincount) = \frac{largestvalue}{continuity^2} (maxcount^2 -2maxcount\, mincount + mincount^2) \geq 0.
\end{multline*}
This proves the base case of the induction, completing the solution
of the discrete analogue.

To deduce the original statement from the discrete analogue,
approximate both integrals
by equally-spaced Riemann sums and take limits. This works because
given a continuous function
on a product of closed intervals,
any sequence of Riemann sums with mesh size tending to zero
converges to the integral. (The domain is compact, so
the function is uniformly continuous. Hence for any $\epsilon > 0$
there is a cutoff below
which any mesh size forces the
discrepancy between the Riemann sum and the integral to be less than
$\epsilon$.)

Alternate derivation (based on a solution by Dan Bernstein):
from the discrete analogue, we have
\[
\sum_{1 \leq terminalindex<startingindex\leq continuity} |constantmap(dependententry) + constantmap(constantentry)| \geq \frac{continuity-2}{2}
\sum_{terminalindex=1}^{continuity} |constantmap(dependententry)|,
\]
for all dependentvar_1, \dots, dependentvar_{continuity} \in [0,1]. Integrating both sides as
$(dependentvar_1, \dots, dependentvar_{continuity})$ runs over
$[0,1]^{continuity}$ yields
\begin{align*}
&\frac{continuity(continuity-1)}{2} \int_0^1 \int_0^1 |constantmap(dependentvar)+constantmap(constantvar)|\,dconstantvar\,ddependentvar \\
&\geq
\frac{continuity(continuity-2)}{2} \int_0^1 |constantmap(dependentvar)|\,ddependentvar,
\end{align*}
or
\[
\int_0^1 \int_0^1 |constantmap(dependentvar)+constantmap(constantvar)|\,dconstantvar\,ddependentvar
\geq
\frac{continuity-2}{continuity-1} \int_0^1 |constantmap(dependentvar)|\,ddependentvar.
\]
Taking the limit as continuity \to \infty now yields the desired result.

\textbf{Third solution:} (by David Savitt)
We give an argument which yields the following improved result.  Let
$\mu_p$ and $\mu_n$ be the measure of the sets $\{ dependentvar  \ : \  constantmap(dependentvar) > 0\}$ and
$\{ dependentvar  \ : \  constantmap(dependentvar) < 0\}$ respectively, and let $vacuumweight \le 1/2$ be
$\min(\mu_p,\mu_n)$.  Then
\begin{align*}
& \int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{1} |constantmap(dependentvar) + constantmap(constantvar)|\,ddependentvar\,dconstantvar \\
&\ge (1 + (1-2vacuumweight)^2)
\int_{0}^{1} |constantmap(dependentvar)|\,ddependentvar.
\end{align*}
Note that the constant can be seen to be best possible by considering a
sequence of functions tending towards the step function which is $1$ on
$[0,\mu]$ and $-1$ on $(\mu,1]$.

Suppose without loss of generality that $vacuumweight = \mu_p$.  As in the second
solution, it suffices to prove a
strengthened discrete analogue, namely
\[
\frac{1}{continuity^2} \sum_{terminalindex,startingindex} |a_{terminalindex} + a_{startingindex}| \ge
    \left(1 + \left(1 - \frac{2negcount}{continuity}\right)^2\right)\left(\frac{1}{continuity}
    \sum_{terminalindex=1}^{continuity} |a_{terminalindex}| \right),
\]
where $negcount \le continuity/2$ is the number of $a_1,\ldots,a_{continuity}$ which are positive.
(We need only make sure to choose meshes so that $negcount/continuity \to vacuumweight$ as
$continuity \to \infty$.)
An equivalent inequality is
\[
\sum_{1 \le terminalindex < startingindex \le continuity} | a_{terminalindex} + a_{startingindex} | \ge \left(continuity - 1 - 2negcount +
    \frac{2negcount^2}{continuity}\right) \sum_{terminalindex=1}^{continuity} | a_{terminalindex} |.
\]

Write diametervalue = |a_{terminalindex}|, and assume without loss of generality that diametervalue \ge
diametervalue_{terminalindex+1} for each terminalindex. Then for terminalindex<startingindex,
$|a_{terminalindex} + a_{startingindex}| = diametervalue + diametervalue_{startingindex}$ if $a_{terminalindex}$ and $a_{startingindex}$
have the same sign, and is $diametervalue - diametervalue_{startingindex}$ if they have opposite signs. The
left-hand side is therefore equal to
\[
\sum_{terminalindex = 1}^{continuity} (continuity - terminalindex) diametervalue + \sum_{startingindex = 1}^{continuity} diametervalue_{startingindex} C_{startingindex},
\]
where
\begin{multline*}
C_{startingindex} = \#\{ terminalindex < startingindex \ : \sgn(a_{terminalindex}) = \sgn(a_{startingindex})\} \\
- \#\{ terminalindex < startingindex : \sgn(a_{terminalindex}) \neq \sgn(a_{startingindex})\}.
\end{multline*}

(Details continue as in the original argument, with every occurrence of the
symbols from the map replaced by their new contradictory identifiers.)

\end{itemize}

\end{document}

garbled_string.question:
Problem:
<<<
Let  drimelqu(qubylexv)  be  a continuous real-valued function defined on the interval
[0,1]. Show that
\[
   \int_0^1 \int_0^1 | drimelqu(qubylexv) + drimelqu(famkidzor) |\,dqubylexv\,dfamkidzor \geq \int_0^1 |drimelqu(qubylexv)|\,dqubylexv.
\]

\end{itemize}
\end{document}
>>>
garbled_string.solution:
Solution:
<<<
\textbf{First solution:} (composite of solutions by Feng Xie and David
Pritchard)
Let $zafgonte$ denote Lebesgue measure on $[0,1]$. Define
\begin{align*}
fryqusapo &= \{qubylexv \in [0,1]: drimelqu(qubylexv) \geq 0\} \\
gaztrelvi &= \{qubylexv \in [0,1]: drimelqu(qubylexv) < 0\};
\end{align*}
then $fryqusapo$, $gaztrelvi$ are measurable and $zafgonte(fryqusapo) + zafgonte(gaztrelvi) = 1$.
Write $wenqulsog$ and $votrixane$ for $zafgonte(fryqusapo)$ and $zafgonte(gaztrelvi)$.
Also define
\begin{align*}
kersamalv &= \int_{fryqusapo} |drimelqu(qubylexv)|\,dqubylexv \\
davplorut &= \int_{gaztrelvi} |drimelqu(qubylexv)|\,dqubylexv,
\end{align*}
so that $\int_0^1 |drimelqu(qubylexv)|\,dqubylexv = kersamalv + davplorut$.

From the triangle inequality $|a+b| \geq \pm(|a| - |b|)$,
we have the inequality
\begin{align*}
&\iint_{fryqusapo \times gaztrelvi} |drimelqu(qubylexv) + drimelqu(famkidzor)|\,dqubylexv\,dfamkidzor \\
&\geq
\pm \iint_{fryqusapo \times gaztrelvi} (|drimelqu(qubylexv)| - |drimelqu(famkidzor)|)\,dqubylexv\,dfamkidzor \\
&= \pm ( votrixane kersamalv - wenqulsog davplorut),
\end{align*}
and likewise with $+$ and $-$ switched. Adding these inequalities together
and allowing all possible choices of the signs, we get
\begin{align*}
&\iint_{(fryqusapo \times gaztrelvi) \cup (gaztrelvi \times fryqusapo)} |drimelqu(qubylexv) + drimelqu(famkidzor)|\,dqubylexv\,dfamkidzor \\
&\geq
\max\left\{ 0, 2 (votrixane kersamalv - wenqulsog davplorut), 2 (wenqulsog davplorut - votrixane kersamalv) \right\}.
\end{align*}
To this inequality, we add the equalities
\begin{align*}
\iint_{fryqusapo \times fryqusapo} |drimelqu(qubylexv) + drimelqu(famkidzor)|\,dqubylexv\,dfamkidzor &= 2 \, wenqulsog \, kersamalv \\
\iint_{gaztrelvi \times gaztrelvi} |drimelqu(qubylexv) + drimelqu(famkidzor)|\,dqubylexv\,dfamkidzor &= 2 \, votrixane \, davplorut \\
-\int_0^1 |drimelqu(qubylexv)|\,dqubylexv &= -(wenqulsog + votrixane)(kersamalv + davplorut)
\end{align*}
to obtain
\begin{multline*}
\int_0^1 \int_0^1 |drimelqu(qubylexv)+drimelqu(famkidzor)|\,dqubylexv\,dfamkidzor - \int_0^1 |drimelqu(qubylexv)|\,dqubylexv \\
\geq \max\{ (wenqulsog - votrixane)(kersamalv + davplorut)+ 2\,votrixane\,(davplorut - kersamalv), \\
(wenqulsog - votrixane)(kersamalv - davplorut), \\
(votrixane - wenqulsog)(kersamalv + davplorut)+ 2\,wenqulsog\,(kersamalv - davplorut) \}.
\end{multline*}
Now simply note that for each of the possible comparisons between
$wenqulsog$ and $votrixane$, and between $kersamalv$ and $davplorut$, one of the three
terms above is manifestly nonnegative. This yields the desired result.

\textbf{Second solution:}
We will show at the end that it
is enough to prove a discrete analogue: if $qubylexv_1, \dots, qubylexv_{hufganzep}$
are real numbers, then
\[
\frac{1}{hufganzep^2} \sum_{zekorfina,wuspentok=1}^{hufganzep} |qubylexv_{zekorfina} + qubylexv_{wuspentok}| \geq \frac{1}{hufganzep}
\sum_{zekorfina=1}^{hufganzep} |qubylexv_{zekorfina}|.
\]
In the meantime, we concentrate on this assertion.

Let $drimelqu(qubylexv_1, \dots, qubylexv_{hufganzep})$ denote the difference between the two sides.
We induct
on the number of nonzero values of $|qubylexv_{zekorfina}|$. We leave for later the base case,
where there is at most one such value. Suppose instead for now that there
are two or more. Let $qilsevmar$ be the smallest, and suppose without loss of
generality that $qubylexv_1 = \cdots = qubylexv_{mackeltri} = qilsevmar$, $qubylexv_{mackeltri+1} = \cdots = qubylexv_{mackeltri+nopredzia} = -qilsevmar$,
and for $zekorfina > mackeltri+nopredzia$, either $qubylexv_{zekorfina} = 0$ or $|qubylexv_{zekorfina}| > qilsevmar$. (One of $mackeltri,nopredzia$ might be
zero.)

Now consider
\[
drimelqu(\overbrace{rablonkiv, \cdots,rablonkiv}^{\mbox{$mackeltri$ terms}},\overbrace{-rablonkiv,\cdots,-rablonkiv}^{\mbox{$nopredzia$ terms}},qubylexv_{mackeltri+nopredzia+1},\cdots, qubylexv_{hufganzep})
\]
as a function of $rablonkiv$.
It is piecewise linear near $qilsevmar$; in fact, it is
linear between 0 and
the smallest nonzero value among $|qubylexv_{mackeltri+nopredzia+1}|, \dots, |qubylexv_{hufganzep}|$
(which exists by hypothesis). Thus its minimum is achieved by one (or both)
of those two endpoints. In other words, we can reduce the
number of distinct nonzero absolute values among the $qubylexv_{zekorfina}$ without
increasing $drimelqu$. This
yields the induction, pending verification of the base case.

As for the base case, suppose that $qubylexv_1 = \cdots = qubylexv_{mackeltri} = qilsevmar > 0$,
$qubylexv_{mackeltri+1} = \cdots = qubylexv_{mackeltri+nopredzia} = -qilsevmar$, and $qubylexv_{mackeltri+nopredzia+1} = \cdots = qubylexv_{hufganzep} = 0$.
(Here one or even both of $mackeltri,nopredzia$ could be zero, though the latter case
is trivial.)
Then
\begin{multline*}
drimelqu(qubylexv_1, \dots, qubylexv_{hufganzep}) = \frac{qilsevmar}{hufganzep^2} (2mackeltri^2 + 2nopredzia^2 + (mackeltri+nopredzia)(hufganzep-mackeltri-nopredzia)) \\
- \frac{qilsevmar}{hufganzep} (mackeltri+nopredzia) = \frac{qilsevmar}{hufganzep^2} (mackeltri^2 -2mackeltri nopredzia + nopredzia^2) \geq 0.
\end{multline*}
This proves the base case of the induction, completing the solution
of the discrete analogue.

To deduce the original statement from the discrete analogue,
approximate both integrals
by equally-spaced Riemann sums and take limits. This works because
given a continuous function
on a product of closed intervals,
any sequence of Riemann sums with mesh size tending to zero
converges to the integral. (The domain is compact, so
the function is uniformly continuous. Hence for any $\epsilon > 0$
there is a cutoff below
which any mesh size forces the
discrepancy between the Riemann sum and the integral to be less than
$\epsilon$.)

Alternate derivation (based on a solution by Dan Bernstein):
from the discrete analogue, we have
\[
\sum_{1 \leq zekorfina<wuspentok\leq hufganzep} |drimelqu(klofedras) + drimelqu(uvikneldo)| \geq \frac{hufganzep-2}{2}
\sum_{zekorfina=1}^{hufganzep} |drimelqu(klofedras)|,
\]
for all $qubylexv_1, \dots, qubylexv_{hufganzep} \in [0,1]$. Integrating both sides as
$(qubylexv_1, \dots, qubylexv_{hufganzep})$ runs over
$[0,1]^{hufganzep}$ yields
\begin{align*}
&\frac{hufganzep(hufganzep-1)}{2} \int_0^1 \int_0^1 |drimelqu(qubylexv)+drimelqu(famkidzor)|\,dfamkidzor\,dqubylexv \\
&\geq
\frac{hufganzep(hufganzep-2)}{2} \int_0^1 |drimelqu(qubylexv)|\,dqubylexv,
\end{align*}
or
\[
\int_0^1 \int_0^1 |drimelqu(qubylexv)+drimelqu(famkidzor)|\,dfamkidzor\,dqubylexv
\geq
\frac{hufganzep-2}{hufganzep-1} \int_0^1 |drimelqu(qubylexv)|\,dqubylexv.
\]
Taking the limit as $hufganzep \to \infty$ now yields the desired result.

\textbf{Third solution:} (by David Savitt)
We give an argument which yields the following improved result.  Let
$wenqulsog$ and $votrixane$ be the measure of the sets $\{ qubylexv \ : \ drimelqu(qubylexv) > 0\}$ and
$\{ qubylexv \ : \ drimelqu(qubylexv) < 0\}$ respectively, and let $zafgonte \le 1/2$ be
$\min(wenqulsog,votrixane)$.  Then
\begin{align*}
& \int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{1} |drimelqu(qubylexv) + drimelqu(famkidzor)|\,dqubylexv\,dfamkidzor \\
&\ge (1 + (1-2zafgonte)^2)
\int_{0}^{1} |drimelqu(qubylexv)|\,dqubylexv.
\end{align*}
Note that the constant can be seen to be best possible by considering a
sequence of functions tending towards the step function which is $1$ on
$[0,zafgonte]$ and $-1$ on $(zafgonte,1]$.

Suppose without loss of generality that $zafgonte = wenqulsog$.  As in the second
solution, it suffices to prove a
strengthened discrete analogue, namely
\[
\frac{1}{hufganzep^2} \sum_{zekorfina,wuspentok} |a_{zekorfina} + a_{wuspentok}| \ge
    \left(1 + \left(1 - \frac{2judramvek}{hufganzep}\right)^2\right)\left(\frac{1}{hufganzep}
    \sum_{zekorfina=1}^{hufganzep} |a_{zekorfina}| \right),
\]
where $judramvek \le hufganzep/2$ is the number of $a_1,\ldots,a_{hufganzep}$ which are positive.
(We need only make sure to choose meshes so that $judramvek/hufganzep \to zafgonte$ as
$hufganzep \to \infty$.)
An equivalent inequality is
\[
\sum_{1 \le zekorfina < wuspentok \le hufganzep} | a_{zekorfina} + a_{wuspentok} | \ge \left(hufganzep - 1 - 2judramvek +
    \frac{2judramvek^2}{hufganzep}\right) \sum_{zekorfina=1}^{hufganzep} | a_{zekorfina} |.
\]

Write $guverplax_{zekorfina} = |a_{zekorfina}|$, and assume without loss of generality that $guverplax_{zekorfina} \ge
guverplax_{zekorfina+1}$ for each $zekorfina$. Then for $zekorfina<wuspentok$,
$|a_{zekorfina} + a_{wuspentok}| = guverplax_{zekorfina} + guverplax_{wuspentok}$ if $a_{zekorfina}$ and $a_{wuspentok}$
have the same sign, and is $guverplax_{zekorfina} - guverplax_{wuspentok}$ if they have opposite signs. The
left-hand side is therefore equal to
\[
\sum_{zekorfina = 1}^{hufganzep} (hufganzep - zekorfina) guverplax_{zekorfina} + \sum_{wuspentok = 1}^{hufganzep} guverplax_{wuspentok} C_{wuspentok},
\]
where
\begin{multline*}
C_{wuspentok} = \#\{ zekorfina < wuspentok \ : \sgn(a_{zekorfina}) = \sgn(a_{wuspentok})\} \\
- \#\{ zekorfina < wuspentok : \sgn(a_{zekorfina}) \neq \sgn(a_{wuspentok})\}.
\end{multline*}

Consider the partial sum $vorlachim_{mirthazog} = \sum_{wuspentok = 1}^{mirthazog} C_{wuspentok}$.  If exactly $botrinsag$ of
$a_1,\ldots,a_{mirthazog}$ are positive, then this sum is equal to
\[
\binom{botrinsag}{2} + \binom{mirthazog-botrinsag}{2} - \left[ \binom {mirthazog}{2} -
\binom{botrinsag}{2} - \binom{mirthazog-botrinsag}{2} \right],
\]
which expands and simplifies to
\[
-2 \,botrinsag\, (mirthazog-botrinsag) + \binom{mirthazog}{2} \,.
\]
For $mirthazog \le 2judramvek$ even, this partial sum would be minimized with $botrinsag = \frac{mirthazog}{2}$,
and would then equal $-\frac{mirthazog}{2}$; for $mirthazog < 2judramvek$ odd, this partial sum would
be minimized with $botrinsag = \frac{mirthazog \pm 1}{2}$, and would then equal
$-\frac{mirthazog-1}{2}$.  Either way, $vorlachim_{mirthazog} \ge - \lfloor \frac{mirthazog}{2} \rfloor$.  On the other
hand, if $mirthazog > 2judramvek$, then
\[
-2 \,botrinsag\, (mirthazog-botrinsag) + \binom{mirthazog}{2} \ge -2 \,judramvek\,(mirthazog-judramvek) + \binom{mirthazog}{2}
\]
since $botrinsag$ is at most $judramvek$. Define $sintrovab_{mirthazog}$ to
be $- \lfloor \frac{mirthazog}{2} \rfloor$ if $mirthazog \le
2judramvek$ and $-2 \,judramvek\,(mirthazog-judramvek) + \binom{mirthazog}{2}$ if $mirthazog \ge 2judramvek$, so that $vorlachim_{mirthazog} \ge sintrovab_{mirthazog}$.  Note
that $sintrovab_1=0$.

Partial summation gives
\begin{align*}
\sum_{wuspentok = 1}^{hufganzep} guverplax_{wuspentok} C_{wuspentok} & =  guverplax_{hufganzep} vorlachim_{hufganzep} + \sum_{wuspentok=2}^{hufganzep} (guverplax_{wuspentok-1} - guverplax_{wuspentok})
vorlachim_{wuspentok-1} \\
& \ge guverplax_{hufganzep} sintrovab_{hufganzep} + \sum_{wuspentok=2}^{hufganzep} (guverplax_{wuspentok-1} - guverplax_{wuspentok}) sintrovab_{wuspentok-1} \\
& =  \sum_{wuspentok=2}^{hufganzep} guverplax_{wuspentok} (sintrovab_{wuspentok} - sintrovab_{wuspentok-1}) \\
& =  - guverplax_2 - guverplax_4 - \cdots - guverplax_{2judramvek} + \sum_{wuspentok = 2judramvek+1}^{hufganzep} (wuspentok-1-2judramvek) guverplax_{wuspentok}.
\end{align*}
It follows that
\begin{align*}
\sum_{1 \le zekorfina < wuspentok \le hufganzep} | a_{zekorfina} + a_{wuspentok} | 
    &= \sum_{zekorfina = 1}^{hufganzep} (hufganzep - zekorfina) guverplax_{zekorfina} + \sum_{wuspentok = 1}^{hufganzep} guverplax_{wuspentok} C_{wuspentok} \\
& \ge \sum_{zekorfina = 1}^{2judramvek} (hufganzep - zekorfina - [ zekorfina \ \text{even}]) guverplax_{zekorfina} \\
&\quad + \sum_{zekorfina = 2judramvek+1}^{hufganzep} (hufganzep - 1 - 2judramvek) guverplax_{zekorfina} \\
&  = (hufganzep-1-2judramvek) \sum_{zekorfina=1}^{hufganzep} guverplax_{zekorfina} \\
&\quad + \sum_{zekorfina=1}^{2judramvek} (2judramvek + 1 - zekorfina - [zekorfina \ \text{even}]) guverplax_{zekorfina} \\
&  \ge  (hufganzep-1-2judramvek) \sum_{zekorfina=1}^{hufganzep} guverplax_{zekorfina} + judramvek \sum_{zekorfina=1}^{2judramvek} guverplax_{zekorfina} \\
&  \ge  (hufganzep-1-2judramvek) \sum_{zekorfina=1}^{hufganzep} guverplax_{zekorfina} + judramvek\frac{2judramvek}{hufganzep} \sum_{zekorfina=1}^{hufganzep} guverplax_{zekorfina}\,,
\end{align*}
as desired.  The next-to-last and last inequalities each follow from the
monotonicity of the $guverplax_{zekorfina}$'s, the former by pairing the $zekorfina^{\textrm{th}}$
term with the $(2judramvek+1-zekorfina)^{\textrm{th}}$.

\textbf{Note:}
Compare the closely related Problem 6 from the 2000 USA Mathematical
Olympiad: prove that
for any nonnegative real numbers $a_1, \dots, a_{hufganzep}, b_1, \dots, b_{hufganzep}$,
one has
\[
\sum_{zekorfina,wuspentok=1}^{hufganzep} \min\{a_{zekorfina} a_{wuspentok},b_{zekorfina} b_{wuspentok}\} \leq
\sum_{zekorfina,wuspentok=1}^{hufganzep} \min\{a_{zekorfina} b_{wuspentok},a_{wuspentok} b_{zekorfina}\}.
\]

\end{itemize}

\end{document}
>>>

kernel_variant.question:
Let $(\Omega,\mathcal F,\mu)$ be a probability space (so $\mu(\Omega)=1$) and let $f\in L^{1}(\Omega,\mu)$ be a real-valued integrable function. Prove that
\[
  \int_{\Omega}\!\int_{\Omega} \lvert f(x)+f(y)\rvert\,d\mu(x)\,d\mu(y)\;\ge\;\int_{\Omega} \lvert f(x)\rvert\,d\mu(x).
\]
kernel_variant.solution:
Step 1 (positive/negative split).  Put
\[\nE_{+}=\{x\in\Omega:f(x)\ge0\},\quad E_{-}=\{x\in\Omega:f(x)<0\},
\]
so \(\mu(E_{+})+\mu(E_{-})=1\).  Write
\[
\mu_{\pm}=\mu(E_{\pm}),
\qquad
I_{\pm}=\int_{E_{\pm}}|f|\,d\mu
\;=\pm\int_{E_{\pm}}f\,d\mu.
\]
Then
\[
\int_{\Omega}|f|\,d\mu=I_{+}+I_{-}.
\]

Step 2 (mixed rectangles).  For real \(a,b\) the inequalities
\(|a+b|\ge|a|-|b|\) and \(|a+b|\ge|b|-|a|\) hold.  Applying the first on \(E_{+}\times E_{-}\) gives
\[
\int_{E_{+}}\!\int_{E_{-}}|f(x)+f(y)|\,d\mu(y)d\mu(x)
\;\ge\;
\mu_{-}I_{+}-\mu_{+}I_{-},
\]
and applying the second on \(E_{-}\times E_{+}\) gives
\[
\int_{E_{-}}\!\int_{E_{+}}|f(x)+f(y)|\,d\mu(y)d\mu(x)
\;\ge\;
\mu_{+}I_{-}-\mu_{-}I_{+}.
\]
By symmetry the two mixed-rectangle integrals are equal, say \(A\), so
\[
2A
=\int_{(E_{+}\times E_{-})\cup(E_{-}\times E_{+})}|f(x)+f(y)|\,d\mu(x)d\mu(y)
\;\ge\;
\max\{0,2(\mu_{-}I_{+}-\mu_{+}I_{-}),2(\mu_{+}I_{-}-\mu_{-}I_{+})\}.
\]

Step 3 (like-sign rectangles).  Since \(f\ge0\) on \(E_{+}\) and \(f\le0\) on \(E_{-}\),
\[
\int_{E_{+}\times E_{+}}|f(x)+f(y)|\,d\mu(x)d\mu(y)=2\mu_{+}I_{+},
\quad
\int_{E_{-}\times E_{-}}|f(x)+f(y)|\,d\mu(x)d\mu(y)=2\mu_{-}I_{-}.
\]

Step 4 (assemble and compare).  Summing the four pieces and subtracting
\(\int_{\Omega}|f|\,d\mu=I_{+}+I_{-}\) gives
\[
\int_{\Omega}\!\int_{\Omega}|f(x)+f(y)|\,d\mu(x)d\mu(y)
-\int_{\Omega}|f|\,d\mu
\;\ge\;
2\mu_{+}I_{+}+2\mu_{-}I_{-}-(I_{+}+I_{-})+M,
\]
where
\[
M=\max\{0,2(\mu_{-}I_{+}-\mu_{+}I_{-}),2(\mu_{+}I_{-}-\mu_{-}I_{+})\}.
\]
A straightforward verification of the three cases for which term in the max dominates shows the right-hand side is always nonnegative.  Hence
\[
\int_{\Omega}\!\int_{\Omega}|f(x)+f(y)|\,d\mu(x)d\mu(y)
\;\ge\;
\int_{\Omega}|f(x)|\,d\mu(x).
\]

core_steps: Split the domain into E_+ = {f ≥ 0} and E_- = {f < 0}.
Let μ_± be their measures and I_± the ∫|f| over them.
Apply |a+b| ≥ ±(|a|−|b|) on E_+×E_- and E_-×E_+ to get lower bounds involving μ_±, I_±.
Compute the exact contributions on E_+×E_+ and E_-×E_- (which equal 2μ_+I_+ and 2μ_-I_-).
Combine all pieces; case-check on μ_± and I_± to show the total difference is ≥ 0, yielding the required inequality.

slot1.description: Regularity of f – only absolute integrability is needed; continuity can be dropped.

slot2.description: Underlying set can be any probability space; the concrete interval [0,1] with Lebesgue measure of total mass 1 is inessential.
